name: aws
context: AWS cloud platform — compute, storage, serverless, databases, IAM, and infrastructure as code
info: The AWS ecosystem covers cloud infrastructure and managed services for building, deploying, and operating applications at scale. This tree is organized around the jobs an agent actually performs: storing files in S3, running serverless functions with Lambda, provisioning infrastructure with CloudFormation, launching EC2 instances, managing databases with RDS, and controlling access with IAM. The AWS SDK for JavaScript v3 is the primary client library, with modular packages per service. All services authenticate via IAM credentials (access key + secret key) scoped to the minimum permissions required.
body:
# AWS Ecosystem

Amazon Web Services (AWS) is a comprehensive cloud platform where agents provision infrastructure, store data, run code, and manage access. AWS offers over 200 services, but this tree focuses on the core building blocks that agents interact with most: S3 for object storage, Lambda for serverless compute, EC2 for virtual machines, RDS for managed databases, IAM for access control, and CloudFormation for infrastructure as code.

## Agent Jobs

The primary tasks an agent performs in this ecosystem:

- **Store files** — upload, download, and manage objects in S3 buckets with configurable access policies. See [[aws/s3]].
- **Run serverless functions** — create, deploy, and invoke Lambda functions without managing servers. See [[aws/lambda]].
- **Provision infrastructure** — define and deploy stacks of AWS resources using CloudFormation templates. See [[aws/cloudformation]].
- **Manage access** — create IAM users, roles, and policies following least-privilege principles. See [[aws/iam]].
- **Launch instances** — start, configure, and terminate EC2 virtual machines for persistent workloads. See [[aws/ec2]].
- **Manage databases** — create and operate RDS managed database instances across multiple engines. See [[aws/rds]].

## Authentication

All AWS services authenticate via IAM credentials. The standard approach uses an access key ID and secret access key, set as environment variables:

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` | IAM access key identifier |
| `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` | IAM secret access key |
| `AWS_REGION` | Target AWS region (e.g. us-east-1) |
| `AWS_SESSION_TOKEN` | Optional temporary session token for assumed roles |

For production workloads, prefer IAM roles over long-lived access keys. Use `sts:AssumeRole` for cross-account access. See [[aws/iam]] for best practices.

## Regions and Availability

AWS operates in multiple geographic regions, each with multiple availability zones:

| Region | Code | Common Use |
|--------|------|------------|
| US East (N. Virginia) | us-east-1 | Default region, broadest service coverage |
| US West (Oregon) | us-west-2 | West coast workloads |
| EU (Ireland) | eu-west-1 | European workloads, GDPR |
| EU (Frankfurt) | eu-central-1 | European workloads, data residency |
| Asia Pacific (Tokyo) | ap-northeast-1 | APAC workloads |
| Asia Pacific (Sydney) | ap-southeast-2 | Australia/Oceania |

## SDK

- **AWS SDK for JavaScript v3** — modular per-service packages (`@aws-sdk/client-s3`, `@aws-sdk/client-lambda`, etc.)
- **AWS CLI** — command-line interface for all AWS services
- **AWS CDK** — infrastructure as code using TypeScript, Python, Java, or Go
- **Boto3** — Python SDK for AWS

sections.title: Core services overview
sections.body: S3 for object storage ([[aws/s3]]), Lambda for serverless compute ([[aws/lambda]]), EC2 for virtual machines ([[aws/ec2]]), RDS for managed databases ([[aws/rds]]), IAM for identity and access control ([[aws/iam]]), CloudFormation for infrastructure as code ([[aws/cloudformation]]). Each service has a dedicated AWS SDK v3 client package.

sections.title: Authentication and credentials
sections.body: All AWS API calls require IAM credentials. Set `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`, and `AWS_REGION` as environment variables. For temporary credentials, add `AWS_SESSION_TOKEN`. Prefer IAM roles over long-lived keys in production. Use `sts:AssumeRole` for cross-account access. See [[aws/iam]] for users, roles, and policies.

sections.title: Regions and availability zones
sections.body: AWS operates in 30+ regions worldwide. Each region has 2-6 availability zones for fault tolerance. Choose regions based on latency, compliance, and service availability. `us-east-1` has the broadest service coverage. Multi-region deployments improve resilience. Use [[aws/cloudformation]] to deploy consistently across regions.

related.note: IAM governs all access to AWS services
CloudFormation is the primary way to provision infrastructure as code
